answer:
Designing SOLID Principles in JS/TS

1. Definition & Context

SOLID is five object-oriented design principles adapted for JavaScript/TypeScript: Single Responsibility (one reason to change), Open/Closed (open for extension, closed for modification), Liskov Substitution (subtypes must be substitutable), Interface Segregation (many specific interfaces over one general), Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions, not concretions). In TypeScript, these apply through interfaces, abstract classes, and dependency injection. In JavaScript, they apply through module boundaries, composition, and duck typing.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the notification module demonstrated all five SOLID principles. SRP: The NotificationService only dispatched notifications — it didn't format messages or query user preferences. OCP: Adding a new channel (Slack) required creating a new class implementing NotificationChannel, not modifying existing email/SMS code. LSP: All channels implemented send(userId, message) and could be swapped interchangeably. ISP: The NotificationChannel interface only had send() — not formatHTML() which only email needed. DIP: The service depended on the NotificationChannel interface, not concrete implementations.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// SOLID notification system (HR Leave System)

// Interface Segregation — minimal interface
interface NotificationChannel {
  send(userId: string, message: string): Promise;
}

// Liskov Substitution — each implementation is interchangeable
class SocketNotification implements NotificationChannel {
  constructor(private io: Server) {}
  async send(userId: string, message: string) {
    this.io.to(`user:${userId}`).emit('notification', { message });
  }
}

class EmailNotification implements NotificationChannel {
  constructor(private mailer: Mailer) {}
  async send(userId: string, message: string) {
    const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: userId } });
    await this.mailer.send({ to: user.email, subject: 'Leave Update', text: message });
  }
}

// Open/Closed — add new channel without modifying existing code
class SlackNotification implements NotificationChannel {
  constructor(private slackClient: WebClient) {}
  async send(userId: string, message: string) {
    const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: userId } });
    await this.slackClient.chat.postMessage({ channel: user.slackId, text: message });
  }
}

// Single Responsibility — only orchestrates, doesn't format or query
// Dependency Inversion — depends on NotificationChannel interface
class NotificationService {
  constructor(private channels: NotificationChannel[]) {}
  
  async notify(userId: string, message: string) {
    await Promise.allSettled(
      this.channels.map(ch => ch.send(userId, message))
    );
  }
}

// Composition root — wire up dependencies
const notificationService = new NotificationService([
  new SocketNotification(io),
  new EmailNotification(mailer),
  new SlackNotification(slackClient)
]);

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: Adding a Slack notification channel caused the entire notification system to fail. When a leave was approved, no notifications were sent at all — not even Socket.io or email.

Investigation: Manoj checked the error logs and found that SlackNotification.send() threw an unhandled error because the Slack API token was misconfigured. The original code used Promise.all(), which rejects if any promise rejects, so the Slack failure cancelled the entire batch.

Root Cause: Violating the robustness principle — one channel's failure shouldn't affect others. The Promise.all() made all channels dependent on each other.

Fix: Changed Promise.all() to Promise.allSettled(), which waits for all promises to complete regardless of rejections. Added error logging for failed channels without blocking successful ones. This also demonstrated proper SRP: the NotificationService handles orchestration and error isolation, not error recovery for individual channels.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Over-abstracting (interface for one implementation)Only abstract when you have 2+ implementations or need testing
God service that does everythingSplit by responsibility: format, send, log, retry
ISP violation: fat interface with unused methodsSplit into focused interfaces; classes implement multiple
DIP overhead: too many layers of abstractionOnly inject dependencies that change or need mocking

7. Validation Strategy

  • SRP test: Each class should have exactly one describe block covering one responsibility
  • OCP test: Add a mock channel without modifying NotificationService — if tests pass, OCP holds
  • LSP test: Run same test suite against each channel implementation
  • Integration test: One channel fails → others still deliver successfully

Debugging SOLID Violations in JS/TS

1. Definition & Context

SOLID violations in JavaScript/TypeScript manifest as tightly coupled modules, god objects, fragile inheritance hierarchies, and code that requires modifying multiple files for a single feature change. Debugging these issues involves identifying code smells: classes with too many responsibilities, switch statements that grow with each new type, mocking nightmares in tests, and circular dependencies.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the LeadService class had grown to 500 lines — it fetched leads, validated forms, formatted display data, sent emails, tracked analytics, and managed pagination state. Adding a new lead source required modifying 4 methods in this single class. This was a classic SRP violation combined with OCP violation (modifying existing code for new features).

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// BEFORE: God class violating SRP + OCP
class LeadService {
  async getLeads(page, filters) { /* 80 lines of query + pagination */ }
  validateLead(data) {
    // OCP violation: switch grows with each new source
    switch (data.source) {
      case 'web': return this.validateWebLead(data);
      case 'referral': return this.validateReferralLead(data);
      case 'linkedin': return this.validateLinkedInLead(data);
      // Must modify this file for every new source!
    }
  }
  formatForDisplay(lead) { /* 40 lines */ }
  sendFollowUpEmail(lead) { /* 30 lines */ }
  trackEvent(event) { /* 20 lines */ }
}

// AFTER: Refactored with SOLID
// SRP: Each class has one job
class LeadRepository {
  async findAll(page: number, filters: LeadFilters): Promise> {
    return prisma.lead.findMany({ where: filters, skip: page * 20, take: 20 });
  }
}

// OCP: Strategy pattern — new sources don't modify existing code
interface LeadValidator {
  canValidate(source: string): boolean;
  validate(data: LeadInput): ValidationResult;
}

class WebLeadValidator implements LeadValidator {
  canValidate(source: string) { return source === 'web'; }
  validate(data: LeadInput) { /* web-specific rules */ }
}

class ReferralLeadValidator implements LeadValidator {
  canValidate(source: string) { return source === 'referral'; }
  validate(data: LeadInput) { /* referral-specific rules */ }
}

// DIP: Orchestrator depends on abstractions
class LeadOrchestrator {
  constructor(
    private repo: LeadRepository,
    private validators: LeadValidator[],
    private notifier: NotificationChannel
  ) {}
  
  async createLead(data: LeadInput) {
    const validator = this.validators.find(v => v.canValidate(data.source));
    if (!validator) throw new Error(`No validator for source: ${data.source}`);
    const result = validator.validate(data);
    if (!result.valid) throw new ValidationError(result.errors);
    const lead = await this.repo.create(data);
    await this.notifier.send(lead.assignedTo, `New lead: ${lead.name}`);
    return lead;
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — CRM Dashboard

Symptom: After adding LinkedIn as a new lead source, the web lead validation broke — web leads started accepting empty company names, which used to be required.

Investigation: Manoj traced the bug to the validateLead method. When adding the LinkedIn case to the switch statement, he accidentally removed the break from the web case, causing fall-through to the LinkedIn validator (which didn't require company name).

Root Cause: OCP violation — modifying existing switch statement code to add new behavior is error-prone. The web validation logic was fragile because it shared a method body with other sources.

Fix: Manoj refactored to the strategy pattern: each lead source got its own validator class implementing LeadValidator. Adding a new source meant creating a new class and registering it in the dependency container — no existing code was modified. The web lead validator's test suite remained green because it was completely isolated from LinkedIn logic.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Switch statements growing with new typesStrategy pattern: map type → handler at registration time
God class accumulates responsibilities over timeReview class size in PR — flag files >200 lines
Circular dependency between refactored servicesExtract shared types to a separate module
Over-refactoring: 50 tiny classes for simple logicOnly split when you have a concrete second use case

7. Validation Strategy

  • File size check: CI warning if any service file exceeds 200 lines
  • Import depth: Lint for circular dependencies with madge
  • Coverage isolation: Each service's tests should need zero mocks of other services
  • New feature test: Adding a new lead source should require zero changes to existing test files

Debugging Clean Architecture Issues

1. Definition & Context

Clean Architecture organizes code into concentric layers: Entities (core business rules), Use Cases (application-specific logic), Interface Adapters (controllers, presenters, gateways), and Frameworks/Drivers (database, web framework, UI). The Dependency Rule states that dependencies must point inward — inner layers know nothing about outer layers. In TypeScript, this means use cases import entity interfaces, not Prisma models directly.

Common violations: importing database models in use cases, framework-specific types leaking into business logic, and tight coupling between route handlers and business rules.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the leave approval logic was initially embedded in the Fastify route handler — 80 lines of business rules mixed with HTTP concerns (parsing params, setting status codes, sending responses). This violated Clean Architecture because changing the approval rules (e.g., adding a 3-level approval chain) required modifying the route handler, risking HTTP logic bugs.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// BEFORE: Business logic in route handler (violation)
fastify.patch('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (request, reply) => {
  const leave = await prisma.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: request.params.id } });
  if (!leave) return reply.code(404).send({ error: 'Not found' });
  if (leave.status !== 'pending') return reply.code(409).send({ error: 'Already processed' });
  // 50 more lines of business logic mixed with HTTP...
});

// AFTER: Clean Architecture separation

// Entity (innermost — no dependencies)
interface Leave {
  id: string;
  status: 'pending' | 'approved' | 'rejected';
  userId: string;
  startDate: Date;
  endDate: Date;
}

// Use Case (depends only on entity interfaces)
interface LeaveRepository {
  findById(id: string): Promise;
  updateStatus(id: string, status: string, approverId: string): Promise;
}
interface BalanceRepository {
  deduct(userId: string, days: number): Promise;
}

class ApproveLeaveUseCase {
  constructor(
    private leaveRepo: LeaveRepository,
    private balanceRepo: BalanceRepository
  ) {}
  
  async execute(leaveId: string, approverId: string): Promise {
    const leave = await this.leaveRepo.findById(leaveId);
    if (!leave) throw new NotFoundError('Leave not found');
    if (leave.status !== 'pending') throw new ConflictError('Already processed');
    
    const days = calculateBusinessDays(leave.startDate, leave.endDate);
    await this.balanceRepo.deduct(leave.userId, days);
    return this.leaveRepo.updateStatus(leaveId, 'approved', approverId);
  }
}

// Adapter: Fastify controller (outermost — maps HTTP to use case)
fastify.patch('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (request, reply) => {
  try {
    const result = await approveLeaveUseCase.execute(
      request.params.id,
      request.user.id
    );
    reply.send(result);
  } catch (err) {
    if (err instanceof NotFoundError) reply.code(404).send({ error: err.message });
    else if (err instanceof ConflictError) reply.code(409).send({ error: err.message });
    else throw err;
  }
});

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: After adding a 3-level approval chain (team lead → department head → HR), the existing unit tests broke even though business logic was unchanged — the tests were tightly coupled to Fastify request/response objects.

Investigation: Manoj found that tests were sending HTTP requests to the route handler and asserting on response codes. To test the new approval chain, he had to mock Fastify's request lifecycle, authentication middleware, and database connections — all for a business logic test.

Root Cause: Business logic was embedded in the route handler, so testing it required the entire HTTP stack. There was no separation between "what the system does" (approve leave) and "how it's triggered" (HTTP PATCH).

Fix: Manoj extracted the approval logic into an ApproveLeaveUseCase class that depended on repository interfaces (not Prisma directly). Unit tests now instantiated the use case with in-memory repository mocks — no HTTP stack needed. The Fastify route handler became a thin adapter that mapped HTTP concepts to use case calls. Tests went from 200ms (with HTTP server startup) to 5ms (pure function calls).

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Prisma types leaking into use casesDefine domain interfaces; map Prisma models in repository adapter
Use case depends on Express/Fastify request objectPass plain DTOs to use case, not framework objects
Over-engineering: 10 files for a CRUD endpointOnly separate when business logic is non-trivial (>20 lines)
Circular dependency between layersUse dependency injection; inner layers define interfaces, outer layers implement

7. Validation Strategy

  • Import lint: Configure ESLint rule — use-case files cannot import from frameworks/
  • Test isolation: Use case tests must run without database, HTTP server, or network
  • Dependency check: madge --circular to verify no circular imports
  • New feature test: Adding approval chain should only touch use-case layer, not route handlers

Comparing Clean Architecture Approaches

1. Definition & Context

Two common approaches to structuring a web application: Clean/Hexagonal Architecture (strict layer separation with dependency inversion) versus Feature-Based/Vertical Slices (organize by feature, each slice owns its entire stack from route to database). Both aim to manage complexity, but they optimize for different things: Clean Architecture optimizes for replaceability (swap frameworks), while Vertical Slices optimize for feature velocity (all related code in one folder).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System vs ContactsHub

Manoj's HR Leave System used Clean Architecture because the business rules were complex (3-level approval, balance calculations, overlap detection) and needed to be testable independently of Fastify. The ContactsHub used Vertical Slices: each feature folder (/contacts, /auth, /import) contained its route, service, model, and validation — no shared abstractions. This made sense because ContactsHub had simple CRUD logic where separating layers would add overhead without benefit.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// Approach A: Clean Architecture folder structure (HR Leave System)
src/
  domain/            // Entities + interfaces (innermost)
    entities/leave.ts
    interfaces/leave-repository.ts
  use-cases/         // Application logic
    approve-leave.ts
    request-leave.ts
  adapters/          // Controllers + repositories
    controllers/leave-controller.ts
    repositories/prisma-leave-repo.ts
  frameworks/        // Fastify setup, Prisma client
    server.ts
    prisma.ts

// Approach B: Vertical Slices folder structure (ContactsHub)
src/
  features/
    contacts/
      contacts.route.ts     // Fastify routes
      contacts.service.ts   // Business logic
      contacts.model.ts     // Mongoose model
      contacts.validation.ts // Zod schema
      contacts.test.ts      // Tests
    auth/
      auth.route.ts
      auth.service.ts
      auth.model.ts
    import/
      import.route.ts
      import.service.ts
      import.worker.ts

5. Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

Symptom: When adding a "merge duplicate contacts" feature to ContactsHub, Manoj found himself duplicating the contact validation logic from contacts.validation.ts in the new merge.validation.ts. Changing a validation rule (e.g., phone format) required updating two files.

Root Cause: Vertical slices don't enforce shared abstractions. Without a domain layer, common validation logic was copied between slices instead of shared.

Fix: Manoj created a shared/ folder for truly shared logic (validation schemas, utility functions) that multiple slices imported. This was a pragmatic middle ground — not full Clean Architecture, but enough structure to prevent duplication. The rule: if logic is used in 2+ features, extract to shared/.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Clean Architecture for simple CRUDOnly use for complex domains; CRUD apps use vertical slices
Vertical slices duplicate shared logicExtract shared/ folder for 2+ feature usage
Team doesn't understand layer boundariesAdd ESLint import restrictions + architecture decision record
Hybrid approach: inconsistent structureDocument in CLAUDE.md which features use which pattern

7. Validation Strategy

  • Manoj's choice: Clean Architecture for HR Leave (complex domain), Vertical Slices for ContactsHub (CRUD)
  • Architecture lint: eslint-plugin-boundaries to enforce import rules
  • Feature test: Adding a new feature in vertical slices should create 1 new folder, not modify existing ones
  • Test speed: Clean Architecture use-case tests should run in <10ms without I/O

Debugging Domain Modeling Issues

1. Definition & Context

Domain modeling is the practice of representing business concepts as code structures: entities, value objects, aggregates, and domain events. Bugs in domain modeling often appear as: anemic domain models (entities are just data containers with all logic in services), primitive obsession (using strings/numbers instead of typed value objects), missing invariants (business rules not enforced in the model), and aggregate boundary issues (modifying related entities without consistency guarantees).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the Leave entity was initially an anemic model — just a Prisma-generated type with no behavior. Business rules like "cannot request leave on weekends," "cannot backdate leaves," and "overlap detection" were scattered across three service methods, leading to inconsistent enforcement. When a new bulk-import feature was added, it bypassed the service and directly inserted via Prisma, creating leaves on weekends.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Rich domain model (HR Leave System)

// Value Object: DateRange — enforces business rules at creation
class DateRange {
  readonly startDate: Date;
  readonly endDate: Date;
  readonly businessDays: number;
  
  private constructor(start: Date, end: Date) {
    this.startDate = start;
    this.endDate = end;
    this.businessDays = this.calculateBusinessDays();
  }
  
  static create(start: Date, end: Date): DateRange {
    if (end <= start) throw new DomainError('End date must be after start date');
    if (DateRange.isWeekend(start)) throw new DomainError('Cannot start leave on weekend');
    if (DateRange.isWeekend(end)) throw new DomainError('Cannot end leave on weekend');
    const now = new Date();
    now.setHours(0, 0, 0, 0);
    if (start < now) throw new DomainError('Cannot backdate leave requests');
    return new DateRange(start, end);
  }
  
  overlaps(other: DateRange): boolean {
    return this.startDate < other.endDate && other.startDate < this.endDate;
  }
  
  private calculateBusinessDays(): number {
    let count = 0;
    const d = new Date(this.startDate);
    while (d <= this.endDate) {
      if (d.getDay() !== 0 && d.getDay() !== 6) count++;
      d.setDate(d.getDate() + 1);
    }
    return count;
  }
  
  private static isWeekend(date: Date): boolean {
    return date.getDay() === 0 || date.getDay() === 6;
  }
}

// Entity: Leave — contains behavior, not just data
class Leave {
  private constructor(
    readonly id: string,
    readonly userId: string,
    readonly dateRange: DateRange,
    private _status: LeaveStatus
  ) {}
  
  static create(userId: string, start: Date, end: Date): Leave {
    const dateRange = DateRange.create(start, end); // Validates at creation
    return new Leave(generateId(), userId, dateRange, 'pending');
  }
  
  approve(approverId: string): LeaveApprovedEvent {
    if (this._status !== 'pending') throw new DomainError('Can only approve pending leaves');
    this._status = 'approved';
    return new LeaveApprovedEvent(this.id, approverId, this.dateRange.businessDays);
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: The bulk leave import (for migrating from an old system) created 15 leave records that fell on weekends. The leave balance calculations were wrong because weekend days were counted.

Investigation: Manoj traced the import code and found it used prisma.leave.createMany() directly, bypassing the service layer that had the weekend validation.

Root Cause: Anemic domain model — business rules (no weekends, no backdating) were in the LeaveService, not in the Leave entity. Any code path that bypassed the service could create invalid data.

Fix: Manoj created a DateRange value object that validated business rules at construction time. The Leave.create() factory method required a valid DateRange, making it impossible to create an invalid leave — even from the import job. All code paths that created leaves had to go through Leave.create(), enforcing invariants at the model level.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Anemic model: all logic in servicesPut invariants in entity/value object constructors
Primitive obsession (dates as strings)Create value objects: DateRange, EmailAddress, PhoneNumber
Bypassing domain model via raw DB accessOnly expose repository methods that accept domain objects
Over-modeling simple CRUD entitiesOnly model entities with complex business rules; simple CRUD = data objects

7. Validation Strategy

  • Invariant test: Attempt to create Leave with weekend dates — assert DomainError thrown
  • Factory test: Leave.create() returns valid entity with correct business day count
  • Overlap test: Create two DateRanges, verify overlaps() returns correct boolean
  • Integration test: Import job uses Leave.create() — invalid rows are rejected with clear errors

Comparing Domain Modeling Approaches

1. Definition & Context

Two approaches to domain modeling in TypeScript: Rich Domain Model (entities encapsulate behavior and enforce invariants — Domain-Driven Design style) versus Transaction Script / Service Layer (entities are plain data objects, all logic in service functions). The choice depends on domain complexity: simple CRUD benefits from transaction scripts (less ceremony), while complex business rules benefit from rich models (invariants enforced everywhere).

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System vs ContactsHub

Manoj's HR Leave System used a Rich Domain Model because leave management had complex invariants: weekend exclusion, balance checks, overlap detection, 3-level approval chains. These rules needed to be enforced consistently across API routes, bulk imports, and scheduled jobs. ContactsHub used Transaction Scripts because contact CRUD had minimal business logic — validate inputs, save to DB, return result. Adding domain objects would have tripled the code with no benefit.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// Approach A: Rich Domain Model (HR Leave System)
class Leave {
  static create(userId: string, start: Date, end: Date, type: LeaveType): Leave {
    const dateRange = DateRange.create(start, end); // Validates invariants
    return new Leave(generateId(), userId, dateRange, type, 'pending');
  }
  
  approve(approverId: string): LeaveApprovedEvent {
    if (this.status !== 'pending') throw new DomainError('Cannot approve');
    this.status = 'approved';
    return new LeaveApprovedEvent(this.id, approverId, this.dateRange.businessDays);
  }
}

// Approach B: Transaction Script (ContactsHub)
async function createContact(data: ContactInput): Promise {
  // Validate with Zod (outside the model)
  const validated = contactSchema.parse(data);
  
  // Transform (phone formatting)
  validated.phone = formatPhone(validated.phone);
  
  // Save directly to MongoDB
  const contact = await Contact.create(validated);
  return contact;
}

async function updateContact(id: string, data: Partial): Promise {
  const validated = contactUpdateSchema.parse(data);
  const contact = await Contact.findByIdAndUpdate(id, { $set: validated }, { new: true });
  if (!contact) throw new NotFoundError('Contact not found');
  return contact;
}

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: After migrating from Transaction Script to Rich Domain Model, the API started returning 500 errors for valid leave requests. The error: "Cannot read property 'create' of undefined" on the DateRange value object.

Investigation: Manoj found that the DateRange class constructor was private (enforcing factory method usage), but the Prisma → Domain mapping code was trying to instantiate it directly using new DateRange() when loading existing leaves from the database.

Root Cause: Rich domain models need a reconstitution path (loading from DB) separate from the creation path (new entities). The factory method DateRange.create() ran validations (no weekends, no backdating), but existing database records might legitimately contain historical weekend leaves from before the rule was added.

Fix: Manoj added a DateRange.reconstitute(start, end) static method that skipped business rule validation — it was used only by the repository when loading from the database. The create() method with full validation was used for new requests. This preserved invariants for new data while allowing historical data to load correctly.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
No reconstitution path for DB loadingSeparate create() (validates) from reconstitute() (trusts DB)
Rich model for CRUD entitiesOnly use rich model when entity has complex behavior
Transaction script rules scatteredGroup related rules in validation functions, document locations
Domain events lost in transaction rollbackCollect events, dispatch only after successful commit

7. Validation Strategy

  • Manoj's choice: Rich model for HR Leave (complex rules), Transaction Script for ContactsHub (simple CRUD)
  • Invariant test: Attempt invalid operations on domain entity — assert DomainError
  • Reconstitution test: Load entity from DB, verify all methods work correctly
  • Rule consistency test: Create entity via API and via import — both enforce same rules
